data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Greg Abel May Create Generational Wealth - My Strategy
data.description:
I wrote a bearish article Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A, BRK.B) in July 2025, as I believed the stock would not perform well after the company announced Warren Buffett would retire. I saw this as an overhang on the stock, and it seems that has been the case: Berkshire Hathaway shares are up just over 1% since that article was published, while the S&P 500 Index (SPY) has gained over 11% in the same time period.

But, now that the transfer of power has occurred, I am turning more positive and think this stock is a hold at this time. Now that Warren Buffett has stepped down as CEO, it has paved the way for Greg Abel to take over and make his mark on the company. Some investors seem to doubt a future without Warren Buffett because the share price has fallen since the start of the year, and this might continue, especially if there is a stock market correction. However, I believe that Greg Abel could be poised to continue providing shareholders with strong gains, which could lead to generational wealth.

The Chart

As shown below, Berkshire Hathaway shares have been trending lower in 2026 and the stock is now near the lows it hit in August and April 2025. This stock is now trading well below the 50-day moving average which is around $499.70 and the 200-day moving average which is about $495.70. Over the past several months, recent attempts to rebound have failed and I believe this stock could need to test the lows it hit in 2025, which was around $450.

Warren Buffett's Tenure Produced Generational Wealth For Long-Time Shareholders

Warren Buffett took control of Berkshire Hathaway back in the 1960's. Over the roughly 60 year span under his control, Berkshire Hathaway shares experienced total cumulative gains of about 6,000,000%, representing a compounded annual gain of around 19.8%. To put it in different terms, every $100 invested since 1965 would now be worth about $6 million. By contrast, $100 invested in the S&P 500 Index (SPY) in 1965 would now be worth about $46,000. The total returns Warren Buffett achieved were life-changing for his long-term shareholders, creating generational wealth in many cases. The chart below shows the growth of $100 invested in the S&P 500 vs. Berkshire Hathaway.

The Greg Abel Era Begins

Greg Abel took the helm as CEO of Berkshire Hathaway on January 1, 2026. It appears that Warren Buffett has great confidence in him due to his track record in deal-making and company management. Greg Abel led Berkshire Hathaway Energy from 2008 to 2018, transforming it into an industry leader. Greg Abel also oversaw several energy sector acquisitions for this company. Since 2018, Greg Abel has managed dozens of companies including BNSF Railway and See's. Now that Greg Abel is CEO, he controls Berkshire Hathaway's record cash hoard of about $382 billion, which some speculate will be used for a major acquisition. He also seems to be moving quickly to make investment changes since Berkshire Hathaway recently revealed it might divest its stake in Kraft Heinz (KHC), which is worth about $7.7 billion. Kraft Heinz has lagged for years, and this might be a sign that Greg will remove non-performing assets from the investment portfolio.

With a background in the energy sector, I expect more energy investments from Greg Abel. On January 2, 2026, Berkshire Hathaway closed a $9.7 billion deal to acquire Oxchem from Occidental Petroleum (OXY). I also expect Berkshire Hathaway to continue expanding its tech investments, especially since its investments in Apple (AAPL) and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) have yielded strong results.

A big question is what Greg Abel will do with the current cash hoard of about $382 billion. There is a belief that this will be used for a major acquisition, but I think this cash level has been built up because valuations are too high in the stock market. Berkshire Hathaway could deploy this cash after a major market correction, setting itself up to buy bargains that will fuel future gains.

It's worth noting that this huge cash reserve could increase pressure on Greg Abel to initiate a dividend. This goes against Warren Buffett's belief, as he correctly thought he could generate more value by investing the cash than by paying out dividends, but Greg Abel might be more open to it. A small dividend could make Berkshire Hathaway stock attractive to fund managers, many of whom can only invest in dividend paying stocks.

The Buffett Indicator Could Be A Stock Market Warning Sign

The "Buffett Indicator" is a popular valuation metric for the stock market. This indicator measures the total value of the U.S. stock markets and compares it to the U.S. Gross Domestic Product, or "GDP." This ratio recently hit 217%, which is beyond the 200% level that Warren Buffett described as "playing with fire." High valuations could explain Berkshire Hathaway's rising cash levels and signal a potential warning sign for stock market investors. To add some historical context for the Buffett Indicator, here are some levels it hit during past major market tops: The Buffett Indicator hit 150% during the Dot-com bubble peak, and reached 105% just before the Great Financial Crisis in 2008. The fair value zone is often considered to be between 75% and 100%. Look at the chart below to see how extreme stock market valuations are today:

Share Buybacks

Berkshire Hathaway last bought back its shares in May 2024. At this time, the stock was trading for about 1.5x to 1.6x book value. But, as the stock price climbed later in 2024 and 2025, the price-to-book ratio rose to between 1.7x to 1.8x. However, the stock is now trading back at around the 1.5x book value level so perhaps share buybacks could be coming in the future. However, I would point out that paying more than book value for this stock is increasingly difficult when the cash on the balance sheet represents such a large percentage of the market cap. The current market cap is around $1.05 trillion, so with about $382 billion in cash, this represents a cash-to-market cap ratio of around 36%.

Based on this calculation, buying Berkshire Hathaway shares now could be considered like paying about $1.50 for every $1 that the company holds on the balance sheet and I don't think that makes sense. It also might not make sense for Berkshire Hathaway because paying $1.50 for $1 in cash, is not accretive.

In 2022, the Biden Administration introduced the Inflation Reduction Act which had a provision in it that levied a 1% excise tax on share buybacks. In a May 2025 shareholder meeting, Warren Buffett commented that this new tax would make it more expensive for the company to buy back shares, than it would be for investors. This is another factor that could be raising the bar for share buybacks, since even a 1% tax would represent a major capital loss for any meaningful buyback.

The Positives I See With Berkshire Hathaway Shares Now

I see the cash hoard as a potential positive because it enables Greg Abel to make his mark on this company and with this war chest of cash, possibly act when a major buying opportunity arises. This dry powder can enable a major acquisition or if the stock market crashes, this cash will allow Greg Abel to go on the offensive and potentially pick up bargains, just as Warren Buffett did during the Great Financial Crisis.

Berkshire Hathaway has a collection of businesses that generate billions in reliable cash flow. This includes companies in the insurance, railroad and energy industries. I believe Warren Buffett likely made a solid choice in choosing Greg Abel as his successor, and that Mr. Abel will follow the investment principles that made Berkshire Hathaway so successful over the decades. I also think it is a positive that Greg Abel is taking action to potentially sell off laggards like Kraft Heinz, and I also believe more investments in the tech sector could be coming. I like that Berkshire Hathaway shows discipline regarding investments and share buybacks, patiently waiting for the right opportunity, which includes buying assets at the right price. Combined with the cash hoard, this could position Berkshire Hathaway to continue providing above average total returns that lead to generational wealth over time.

The Potential Risks I See With Berkshire Hathaway

I see the cash as a positive, but it could become a negative anchor if it is not deployed within a reasonable timeframe. The returns on cash invested in Treasury Bills or money market funds typically generate around 4% these days, which isn't enough to achieve the historical returns that made Berkshire Hathaway what it is today. Berkshire Hathaway is so large now that finding major acquisition targets or investment opportunities could be a challenge.

In 2025, Berkshire Hathaway shares rose by about 10%, but this significantly trailed the S&P 500 Index's gains of about 16%. This can't go on too long, because it will raise concerns that this company is not what it used to be. I also see some concentration risks since Berkshire Hathaway has significant exposure to Apple and American Express (AXP). If these stocks decline, they could pull down the share price.

Here's My Buying Strategy

I am warming up to buying this stock at some point, but I still feel it is a hold for a number of reasons. One issue is that the stock market is highly valued right now, and I am concerned that with rising unemployment, the economy could head toward a recession. If there is an economic slowdown, there will likely be a significant stock market correction, which might be the next major buying opportunity. I can't help but think that this might be why Berkshire Hathaway's cash hoard has grown to record levels. Before buying a significant position in this stock, I'm looking for a lower entry point and/or some other event to occur.

To determine when to buy this stock, I think it makes sense to seek guidance from the Buffett Indicator and from Berkshire Hathaway as well. If the Buffett Indicator went back towards the "fair value" level of below 100%, I would likely buy this and many other stocks. However, unless there is a stock market plunge, we might not see that level hit anytime soon. So, the other event that could entice me to buy shares is if Berkshire Hathaway announces it is buying back shares. Considering the cash this company has available for buybacks, it doesn't make sense for me to buy this stock, if the company seems to prefer holding cash over purchasing its own shares. I believe stock buybacks could be the ultimate buy signal for Berkshire Hathaway and I will patiently wait for this to occur.

In Summary

I believe that Berkshire Hathaway has the potential to continue producing the type of long-term total returns that lead to generational wealth. However, I believe that it makes sense to wait for a better buying opportunity, which could come during a market correction. I plan to take my cues on when to buy BRK stock from the company itself; I will start buying shares when the company begins using its cash for share repurchases.

data.title: BigBear.ai Broadens Product Line To Tap Larger Addressable Market
data.description:
Thesis: BigBear is looking to diversify its current portfolio

BigBear.ai (BBAI) stock has struggled to push past the $7 mark this year and has found itself floating around the high $5 range. I think in terms of growth trajectory, the company is still at a very early stage, and whilst their product line is really quite impressive, we need to see them start converting contacts to actual revenue. So with the stock price now trading at that level, I still think there is more upside to this stock in 2026. This is especially true with regard to the growth of their military-related contacts, which make up the bulk of their business. I last covered BigBear back in mid-December. The company has just made a pretty strategic but very risky move into the UAE. However, despite the impressive portfolio we're seeing amid the global expansion push, BigBear is making a push to diversify its existing products into offerings with a bigger market. Many of their AI systems are really quite unique in that they need a specific environment to work in, but last year and in early 2026, the company started to show signs of portfolio diversification. Via the acquisition of CargoSteer's AI scanning tech and also the integration of LightWave Radar into their existing ConductorOS system, BigBear is showing me the right signs.

A push into customs contacts

As you know, BigBear has recently announced that they’re set to acquire a number of valuable assets from CargoSeer Technologies. I think it’s a pretty important move and one that is more focused on getting a better footprint in AI-based border security/trade enforcement. The assets themselves are certain technologies, including some of CargoSeer’s advanced cargo inspection and risk management capabilities. Specifically, they’re getting an AI Shipment Inspection Platform, which is a specialized artificial-intelligence system built to enhance Non-Intrusive Inspection (NII) of cargo containers. As you can imagine, this is very useful for customs and border agencies, where time is everything when managing ports of entry. Now, the AI system works by combining automated image analysis, computer vision, machine learning, and trade-data fusion into just a single decision-support tool. So at its core, the platform is essentially using computer vision and deep-learning models to analyze X-ray images or other sensor data from cargo containers. However, the system can also automatically interpret any complex visual patterns to detect the usual things they’d want to find, such as anomalies, concealed objects, or just irregular shapes that may indicate security threats.

Now, the aim here is to reduce reliance on slow and error-prone manual review, and what makes the technology particularly powerful is that it does not evaluate the cargo images in isolation, but instead, it fuses visual inspection results with all the trade/cargo intelligence, such as shipping manifests. So it also takes into account bills of lading, commodity codes, carrier histories, routing patterns, and origin-destination data to output a range of risk scores for each shipment. Keep in mind this isn't something that will replace manual inspection, but rather it allows operators to prioritize the highest-risk containers for physical inspection. In terms of processing speed, the platform is pretty quick and is tailored towards high-throughput environments like major ports of entry. It's tipped to process scans in seconds rather than minutes and significantly increase inspection capacity without actually slowing trade.

Now, the interesting thing here is that rather than a full corporate acquisition, BigBear is only purchasing those key technologies. Management also emphasized they want the intellectual property and operational tools that enhance Non-Intrusive Inspection via automation. So I think this should strengthen BigBear’s portfolio when it comes to border agencies. They now have a very capable tool to help customs agencies with the product, marketed mainly towards reducing bottlenecks at ports of entry. I also think it’s a good time to be diversifying into this area, since bottlenecks are now an increasingly critical challenge, as global trade volumes have been rising. From a business perspective, the acquisition is all about expanding BigBear’s market, especially within government and border enforcement programs, which make up the bulk of its revenue. And the good thing about customs contracts is that they can potentially be long-term opportunities.

ConductorOS AI orchestration

BigBears ConductorOS system has been around for a couple of years now, but only recently have we seen the company step up their push to make this system more appealing. You see, at its core, the system is a pretty advanced AI orchestration platform that was designed to transform how distributed AI, data, and sensor systems operate. This is especially useful in areas like mission-critical defense, national security, and edge computing environments. And that's because ConductorOS can give a dynamic orchestration layer that harmonizes disparate hardware, sensors, and data streams; it can even bring together AI/ML models so they can all run together seamlessly. The reality is that military equipment is spread out across geographic locations, and some are left in very low or intermittent bandwidth conditions. So it leaves the operator needing some kind of real-time inference and autonomous decision-making system that can feed data from all sources directly at the edge rather than having to rely on distant cloud processing, which can take time.

As a quick example, imagine a forward operating base that is being protected from drone attacks. The military already has multiple sensors deployed around the base; things from radar, infrared cameras, acoustic sensors, and even other surveillance drones are there to keep an eye on the base. It leaves ConductorOS to create an orchestration layer connecting all of them together. So eventually, when a small drone enters the airspace, the radar detects an object, then an infrared camera picks up a heat signature, and finally an acoustic sensor hears its motor. Those are three different data points that ConductorOS fuses together in real time and runs AI models almost instantly at the edge to classify the object as a hostile drone rather than a friendly aircraft. And what's more is that it can then automatically prioritize the threat and alert operators with a single unified track instead of having to go through multiple raw sensor feeds. Keep in mind that all of this happens in seconds without needing to send data back to a distant cloud server. You see, one of the biggest pushes from the U.S. military in recent times is to get commanders to move from detection to decision at machine speed, with the help of new tech, and it’s still quite early days in terms of adoption.

The good thing is that ConductorOS has already been deployed and showcased in some real-world environments. We’ve seen it in some U.S. Department of Defence experimentation events like the RDER T-REX24-2 exercise, and also the Navy has taken an interest in it with its inclusion in Mission Autonomy Proving Ground. The tech has even been tipped for deployment with AUKUS forces during Exercise Talisman Sabre 2025. That gives you a sense of just how early adoption is with such a system. Now, the ease of getting this system into a military environment got a lot easier back when it was flagged as 'awardable' via the DoD’s Tradewinds procurement platform. So I think that should help with its adoption in defense contracting. In terms of revenue, ConductorOS itself is included in BigBear’s government deployments section, so a contract win would contribute indirectly to the company’s revenue growth through government and allied military engagements.

AI-border security push

Even though adoption of ConductorOS is still quite early, I think it’s a good thing we’re still seeing BigBear try to diversify the portfolio in the sense of increasing the market for the tech. Back in December, BigBear announced a pretty hefty partnership deal with C Speed centers. The goal here is to integrate BigBear’s own ConductorOS platform along with C Speed’s software-defined LightWave Radar system. Now, that sounds like a lot of fancy words, but the ultimate goal here is just to create a more intelligent, autonomous border-security/defense solution. One that’s capable of detecting, classifying, and then responding to threats in real time across air, ground, or sea domains. Essentially, the core of the ConductorOS system is still here doing its job, but rather than simply adding analytics to existing sensors to integrate them, this new partnership embeds AI directly at the radar level. That should enable machine-speed processing of the raw radar data to identify suspicious objects and then immediately trigger prioritized alerts along with recommended responses for the operators.

So, to break the tech down by contribution, the fusing of data from the multiple radar units/sensors is still being done by ConductorOS, but the multi-domain orchestration is then given to the LightWave Radar to then dynamically shift operational modes. Now, shifting operational modes could be something like switching from airborne surveillance to maritime or ground threat detection immediately without manual reconfiguration. Think of the LightWave Radar as the sensor backbone in the partnership; it’s the system that can actually see the physical world. Whilst BigBear’s ConductorOS is playing the role of the AI brain that interprets what it sees and decides what to do next. LightWave itself is only a software-defined radar, so its behavior isn’t controlled by fixed hardware, meaning it can be rapidly reconfigured to look for different kinds of threats without having to change the physical system. Now, you might think that role is all a bit too simple, and surely the military has software already in place similar to this. But what makes LightWave especially valuable in this partnership is that it can dynamically change modes based on AI input. For example, if ConductorOS determines a detected object is an airborne drone rather than a surface vehicle, the radar will then automatically shift into an airborne-tracking mode optimised for that specific threat. So the LightWave Radar is an extremely flexible, multi-mission sensing platform that can just keep feeding raw radar data continuously into ConductorOS.

I think this kind of integration will make the overall package much more attractive for border patrol, homeland security, and eventually defense missions. On one hand, you’re reducing operator workload via that automated reporting, and on the other, the time until a response can be made to the threat is shortened considerably since all the data is being interpreted and handed to you on a plate. It should also open up the door for BigBear to get deeper military contacts, so things like use in counter-UAS, air and missile defense, and hopefully border monitoring missions, since this area has a huge market.

The risk: valuation is trading on expectations

When it comes to a valuation, BigBear currently has a market cap of about $2.54 billion with an enterprise value of $2.07 billion. It’s a fair reflection of the balance sheet that includes $586.7 million in cash against just $112.9 million in total debt. Now, based on its valuation multiples, the stock is priced at about 12.6x price to sales and about 14.3x enterprise value to sales, which would suggest an annual revenue of roughly $145 million on a trailing basis. These multiples are also far higher than the Information Tech sector median, which tells me investors are assigning a premium to future growth potential rather than what we’re seeing now on current earnings power. The truth is that while BigBear’s product portfolio looks very appealing, we need to see contract growth turn into revenue to justify that premium. As you know, the company does not yet have any meaningful price-to-earnings ratios since earnings remain negative. Their valuation is driven primarily by revenue expectations, and the potential of long-term profitability should adoption step up for its AI platforms/government contracts. It adds to the overall risk of buying in now at the mid- to high-$5 level.

Looking ahead

In terms of next earnings, we can expect Bigbear to report 4Q25 sometime around March 5th after market close. For that quarter, analysts have forecasted revenue to fall in the range of about $33.3 million and another loss per share at roughly –$0.06. It would be continued negative earnings and also relatively stable sales compared with the recent quarters we’ve seen. For FY25, Wall Street is expecting annual revenue of around $133.7 million with an EPS loss of about –$0.93, which, if realised, I think is a reflection of ongoing investment in growth.

data.title: Construction Partners: Working Hard To Achieve Real Things
data.description:
Shares of Construction Partners (ROAD) have seen continued gains. This came after I concluded that the company was paving the road for continued growth at the end of 2024.

This was driven by solid growth, strategic acquisitions, and a strong position in the Sunbelt states, yet valuations were simply too demanding for me to get involved at a 50 times earnings multiple.

Forwarding a little over a year, the company continues to deliver on savvy M&A and strong organic growth, and while shares have seen impressive gains, multiples have come down meaningfully. This is to be applauded, as the business has much larger ambitions, making me more upbeat on the business and investment; I'm just awaiting better entry levels to get involved.

Built From Scratch

Construction Partners was purposely built in 2001, based on a 3-point thesis. These points include partnering with experienced operators, offering infrastructure services to meet essential needs, and focusing on fragmented industries with a long runway for growth.

The market for asphalt is huge, with 94% of U.S. roads made from asphalt. These roads are, on average, in pretty mediocre shape while requiring regular maintenance. Continued increases in traffic numbers and increased weight of vehicles contribute to higher wear of these roads. Furthermore, the company apparently focuses on the Sunbelt states, which enjoy above-average population growth.

By now the business employs nearly 7,000 workers, who operate in 8 states, including the great state of Texas as well as a range of states in the southeastern parts of the nation. The company has over a hundred hot mix asphalt plants, 17 aggregate facilities, as well as some AC terminals.

The company went public in 2018 at levels in the low teens, as the company has made numerous acquisitions to expand the business over time. Still just a $25 stock in 2023, real momentum pushed shares up to a high around $100 late in 2024 to a high near $140 in 2025, with shares now trading hands at $110 per share.

Over the past decade, the company has about five-folded sales while expanding mid-single-digit operating margins to 10% of sales, as such growth numbers are impressive enough to tolerate about 25% dilution of the shareholder base over time.

Back to 2024

Towards the end of 2024, the company reported a 17% increase in full-year sales to $1.82 billion, as operating profits came in at $111 million, with earnings reported at $1.31 per share. That thesis was about to change, with further growth seen into 2025, following a larger $900 million deal announced for Asphalt Inc.

This company is an asphalt manufacturing mix and pavement business in Texas, with the deal set to add over half a billion in sales while posting EBITDA margins in the low 20% range.

Pro forma net debt was seen up to $1.1 billion, for a leverage ratio around 3.2 times, with pro forma EBITDA seen at $340 million. That was a bit elevated, as the contribution was seen to the 2025 results, with revenues seen around $2.53 billion (with that guidance incorporating 11 months of contribution from Asphalt Inc). Earnings were seen at a midpoint of $105 million, and with a share count of 56 million shares, earnings were seen just short of $2 per share.

Trading at $89, I believed that shares were too hot to handle at a near 50 times earnings multiple, while some leverage was apparent. While I liked the M&A structure, which is the issuance of expensively priced shares, I simply feared the set-up, given the valuations.

2025 - Delivering

Supported by the purchase of Asphalt, bolt-on M&A, and strong performance of the business, Construction Partners has posted strong results at the start of 2025. First-quarter sales rose 42% to $561 million. Such momentum made it so that the full-year sales guidance was upped to $2.70 billion, with adjusted earnings seen at $116 million.

In the second quarter, sales were up more than 50%. The full-year revenue guidance was hiked by a hundred million to $2.80 billion, with adjusted earnings now seen as high as $128 million, as momentum continued in the second half of the year as well.

In the end, 2025 sales rose as much as 54% to $2.81 billion, with adjusted EBITDA seen up to $424 million and adjusted earnings seen at $122 million. That number, even as it came in a bit softer than previously guided, looks quite clean and comes in at $2.17 per share, way ahead of my working estimate of just shy of $2 per share late in 2024.

Net debt was reported at $1.45 billion. Leverage was seen up to 3.4 times, as the total amount spent on M&A came in at $1.15 billion in the fiscal year 2025, with more bolt-on M&A deals announced during the year. This is not a major concern, as the company issued a convincing 2026 outlook, which calls for sales between $3.4 and $3.5 billion, aided by some larger M&A deals announced at the start of the fiscal year.

Adjusted earnings are seen at a midpoint of $161 million, close to $3 per share, all while EBITDA is seen around $530 million, for a 2.7 times leverage ratio. However, that might not necessarily be achieved, as the two most recent bolt-on deals were announced at the start of the fiscal year 2026 and thus have not been paid for yet by 2025.

What Now?

With 56 million shares now trading up to $110 per share, the market value of the firm stands at nearly $6.2 billion, for a $7.6 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at just over 2 times sales, about 14-15 times EBITDA, as earnings multiples have come in a bit, but still come in at a high 30 earnings multiple.

Investors do not mind paying this multiple, which actually has come down, as the company announced ROAD 2030 targets, with roughly 30 basis points of margin expansion seen each year. Achieving that would imply EBITDA doubling more or less from current performance. M&A is key, as the acquisitions of 2025, which now make a full-year contribution, and deals announced early in 2026 are combined to be seen contributing around $450 million to 2026 sales. This suggests a high single-digit increase in organic sales, as discussed on the conference call.

Frankly, my regards for the business have increased here, as shares have been lagging the markets a bit. Given all this, I am becoming more constructive, with multiples having come in a bit, and the ambitions for the business being rather high and achievable. This makes me quite interested to keep a closer eye on the business, its growth, and M&A moves in 2026, with a real intention to get involved in serious dips here.

data.title: V.F. Corp.: The Turnaround Is Advancing
data.description:
V.F. Corporation (VFC) reported the company’s fiscal Q3 results from the October-December period on the 28th of January. The apparel giant showed major turnaround progress as organic sales growth finally turned positive after the divestment of Dickies. While the performance remains inconsistent across brands and markets, I believe that V.F.’s overall turnaround outlook is now good.

The market reacted with a sharp -10% stock decline as of writing despite V.F.’s good results, offsetting some recent stock gains. I don't believe the decline to be reflective of V.F.'s results.

I remained at a Hold rating in my previous September 2025 article on the stock, titled “V.F. Corp.: A Bullish $220 Million Loss On Dickies.” The stock has since returned 22%, outperforming the S&P 500’s (SP500) 5% return very clearly even after the post-earnings selloff.

V.F.’s Turnaround is Advancing Despite Market & Brand Volatility

V.F. reported fiscal Q3 results that clearly exceeded expectations. Total revenues came in at $2.88 billion, beating Wall Street’s consensus by $120 million. The adjusted EPS of $0.58 beat the consensus estimate by $0.14.

Adjusting for the pending divestment of Dickies, V.F. has now returned to positive revenue growth—excluding Dickies, revenues came in at $2.82 billion, up by 2% year-on-year in constant currency. Strength was driven by good 5% growth in The North Face and Timberland’s revenue, driven by the brands’ increasing relevance within the Americas. While Vans again showed a massive year-on-year decline in revenue, this time at -10%, the brand’s decreasing share of overall sales has led V.F.’s total growth to inflect to a positive level.

V.F. guides organic Q4 revenue growth at 0-2%, suggesting that the performance will remain more balanced in the upcoming quarter. The report marks a clear trend shift in V.F’s turnaround story is that the company has finally stabilized its overall sales performance at positive growth.

Americas showed 6% constant currency revenue growth; meanwhile, revenue declined by -3% in EMEA and by -4% in APAC—V.F.’s performance is still quite inconsistent by market. I don’t believe the mixed market outlook matters much; the overall sales performance matters the most.

Going forward, the outlook is also inconsistent by brand—The North Face and Timberland are clearly showing strength, especially in the U.S., but Vans’ brand turnaround still hasn’t shown meaningful progress. Vans’ turnaround is becoming increasingly unlikely as quarters go on without any stabilization in the brand’s sales. The strategies that were outlined for The North Face and Timberland in V.F.’s Investor Day Part 2 are clearly working. So far, my assessment of the event is holding up relatively well, and The North Face’s momentum has even been a positive surprise.

Third quarter revenue growth was driven by a healthy 3% increase in direct-to-consumer, in part slowed by a -1% decline in wholesale. I believe that the channel mix gives positive signals regarding V.F.’s outlook—the direct-to-consumer channel signals sales trends better over the short term than wholesale, as wholesale shipments can vary significantly on a quarterly basis. Direct-to-consumer also typically comes with much higher gross margins, making the slight shift in channel mix positive.

V.F.’s positive momentum carried onto profitability as well. Cost savings across the organization led to a 10 basis point gain in the gross margin and a 20 basis point margin gain from lower SG&A. Adjusted operating income came in at $341 million, up by 7% year-on-year when excluding Dickies. Such gross margin stability is especially notable in the current U.S. tariff landscape; V.F. managed to more than offset approximately $40 million in tariff pressure, around 140 basis points of revenue.

V.F. is Roughly Fairly Valued, but Volatile

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for V.F. stock. I now already exclude Dickies’ financials in FY2026 estimates, given that V.F. also reports results without the brand. I again add $600 million in cash to V.F. from the divestment.

As previously, I estimate V.F.’s improving brand mix to stabilize growth at 2% from FY2028 forward—a weak Vans, but strong The North Face and Timberland make the overall outlook fair. I have raised revenue expectations for FY2027, though, given that The North Face’s momentum has already improved significantly.

I again assume tariff mitigation and cost savings to improve V.F.’s profitability noticeably to an 8.2% adjusted EBIT margin. Strict CapEx spending makes V.F.’s cash flow outlook good.

The estimates put V.F.’s fair value at $18.3, near the stock price as of writing. The stock appears roughly fairly valued given the company’s improved sales momentum and better margin prospects. It still has to be noted that the DCF valuation is very sensitive to small changes in financial assumptions, as V.F.’s debt-heavy balance sheet causes significant leverage in shareholder value. Especially due to brand volatility, I believe that V.F. is likely to be a volatile investment going forward.

The fair value estimate is up from $12.9 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.44%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 3.83% interest rate, annualized from Q3 debt and interest expenses. I again estimate a fairly high 35% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.25% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.42. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 10.39% and the WACC at 8.44%.

Takeaway

The bottom line remains similar after V.F.’s Q3 results; The North Face and Timberland are showing great strength, mostly offset by Vans’ weakness. The overall growth outlook has now improved to a fair level following the divestment of Dickies, and V.F.’s turnaround efforts are improving the margin outlook through aggressive cost savings. The market didn’t seem to appreciate V.F’s results, but the negative reaction seems to merely be a correction after the stock previously showed strong gains. I remain at a Hold rating for VFC stock.

data.title: Vistra: Pullback Driven By Accounting Optics, Not Fundamentals
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Luciano Rahal as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

I feel the pullback in Vistra Corp.'s (VST) stock price was driven by accounting optics, not fundamentals. I believe Vistra is a buy due to several reasons that include (1) a historical and disciplined hedging and contracting strategy that the market has continued to ignore and misprice as volatile earnings despite earnings visibility through 2027; (2) an evolving and long-standing capital allocation framework planning to deploy ~$10 billion of cash through aggressive share buybacks, balance sheet deleveraging, and critical investments in smart acquisitions and high-return plants; and (3) an increasing energy demand by hyperscalers and AI in key markets like the Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT), a non-profit grid operator, and the Pennsylvania–New Jersey–Maryland Interconnection (PJM), a non-profit regional transmission organization, positions Vistra’s diversified generation fleet to capture this growth.

Company Overview

Vistra Corp. is an independent power producer located in the U.S. that combines a large retail platform with a diversified power generation fleet. The company operates in key markets like ERCOT and PJM and serves millions of customers while hedging its load with its broad generation fleet.

Fundamental Analysis

Share prices were crushed following Q3 earnings, dropping ~25%, driven by a huge GAAP accounting loss rather than actual deterioration in fundamentals. A ~$1.7 billion unrealized loss on non-cash energy hedges was reported, and a fire at Martin Lake blurred otherwise impressive performance. However, management expects to "recover a majority of the expenditures associated" with the fire through property damage insurance and has already recognized $104 million of recoveries. On the accounting side, the unrealized loss should not affect earnings, as a significant part of the hedge contracts should reverse as they roll into delivery.

Vistra has continued to sell off following its earnings due to the lack of announcements of hyperscaler or data center deals, which represents investor fixation on near-term deal announcements and timing rather than an actual deterioration of underlying demand in ERCOT and PJM, where utility forecasts continue to call for higher load growth based on utility and retail load forecasts.

Management, at the same time, upgraded full-year guidance by estimating adjusted EBITDA to hit the midpoint estimates ($5.7-5.9 billion). They also have introduced strong adjusted EBITDA growth for 2026 at ~20% and FCF growth up ~50% from 2024, reinforcing my view that earnings visibility is not being threatened and instead the pullback reflects a market overreaction to accounting standards rather than actual deterioration in the company’s fundamentals.

Competitive Landscape

Vistra is in the merchant IPP sector that includes companies like NRG Energy, Inc. (NRG), The AES Corporaion (AES), and Constillation Energy Corporation (CEG), but Vistra is better positioned than the rest. For example, AES is strongly regulated and has many long-term PPAs, which gives it low merchant exposure if energy prices rise. Constellation Energy is focused more on the nuclear generation business and maintains strong hedging discipline. NRG, on the other hand, is the most comparable peer to Vistra, due to its hybrid generation and retail business model; however, NRG continues to maintain a short-term focused hedging strategy that leads to greater earnings sensitivity due to power price movements. Unlike other merchant IPPs that hedge their energy, Vistra has an outstanding history of disciplined energy hedging and has been leading the sector in hedging coverage with 96% and 70% of expected power generation hedged for 2026 and 2027, respectively. Vistra is currently priced as if it generates unreliable earnings, which is not an issue due to its constant and ever-improving hedging strategy, creating a disconnect between actual and perceived risk.

Recent developments and catalysts

Vistra is entering the next several years with multiple identifiable catalysts and trends that will improve its earnings potential and visibility. First overall, ERCOT is forecasting energy to grow at a very accelerated rate, with demand increasing by over ~30 GW from 2026 to 2028, driven by hyperscalers and data centers, while PJM is projected to grow at ~5% per year for the next 10 years. This is going to position Vistra’s new acquisitions such as the Cogentrix portfolio of ~5.5 GW of modern natural gas generation for ~$4 billion across PJM, ERCOT, and Independent System Operation (ISO), another non-profit grid operator, and the ~$1.9 billion acquisition of ~2.6 GW of gas-fired assets from the Lotus Infrastructure portfolio in May 2025 to capture all of this demand growth with out having to spend much more on building new plants.

Vista’s 1.2 GW PPA with Comanche Peak, announced September 19, 2025, is supposed to start contributing to profitability post 2026 and will last over 20 years, and will also help make their great earnings visibility even stronger with those cash flows lasting for a long time. Management has also announced a strong capital allocation framework with plans to deploy ~$10 billion of cumulative cash between the end of 2025 and 2027 on strong share buybacks, balance sheet deleveraging, and more high return investments and acquisitions. VST has previously bought back over ~30% of shares outstanding since 2021, representing management's confidence, and they have no plans on slowing down with an additional ~$1 billion planned annually for 2026 and 2027.

They also recently announced a partnership with Meta to provide them with up to 6.6 GW of nuclear power by 2035 for its data centers. This PPA allows Meta to purchase power from three nuclear power plants located in Pennsylvania and Ohio, and is expected to start with 2,176 MW of operating generation beginning later this year. Any other hyperscaler or data center deals with long-term PPAs will help improve investor sentiment, which has been falling due to a lack of deals announced since June/July.

The Trump administration has, however, recently outlined a proposal that would require large technology companies to pay for the construction of new power plants in PJM, instead of just relying on existing generation like they have been doing. They are also exploring the limits on how much existing plants can charge in the PJM capacity markets. These plans aim to address the increasing electricity prices and reliability during peak hours driven by rapid data center growth, but it would affect IPP generators by reducing the pricing power they have in the PJM market. This announcement is just a proposal and does not represent binding policy, but with the political pressure the PJM market faces it really does increase the likelihood of market changes, which makes this a near-term negative catalyst that could affect future cash flows.

Growth

Vistra is entering a period of structural growth due to the visible electricity demand driven by hyperscalers, data centers, and broader electrification themes in its core markets. This demand is expected to be capitalized by VST's already strong standing generation fleet and recent acquisitions like the Lotus and Cogentrix portfolios, which are expected to be accretive as they immediately add cash generation and dispatchable capacity without requiring significant up-front greenfield capital investment.

Valuation

The valuation reflects higher revenues in ERCOT and PJM due to demand growth, added EBITDA from the Lotus and Cogentrix acquisitions starting in 2026, long-term nuclear PPAs, and ongoing share repurchases. My price target of $201 comes from a two-stage DCF with the terminal value being calculated using EV/EBITDA of 8.5x. While Vistra has historically traded around 10.2x EV/EBITDA over the past two years, I apply a conservative lower multiple to reflect Vistra’s continued exposure to merchant generation and softening pricing power in ERCOT and PJM in the terminal years as supply starts catching up to demand, even though peers have historically traded around 9.5–11x. The DCF also assumes a conservative 11.4% WACC with the terminal value comprising ~53% of PV enterprise value. My price target is further supported by capacity and distribution metrics, with Vistra trading at $1.8 million per MW and $354 thousand per GWh versus the peer mean of ~$2.6 million per MW and ~$801 thousand per GWh, showing the market continues to value the company as a lower-quality, higher-volatility merchant generator despite being quite frankly the opposite. My price target reflects a 6 to 12 month investment horizon, as I expect continued strong execution and earnings visibility to help close the valuation gap.

Key Risks

While Vistra's long-term outlook remains very strong and stable, there are a few key risks it faces that could impact its earnings visibility and further deteriorate investor sentiment. While energy demand is projected to increase at a rapid pace in the U.S., not all announced hyperscaler projects may reach completion, and AI workloads have been getting more efficient, as seen in DeepSeek, which would lower projected load growth.

However, because the majority of 2026 and 2027 volumes are already hedged, the financial impact of weaker load growth would damage forecasted cash flows beyond 2027. Vistra manages a staggering and growing ~200 TWh annually, which requires constant hedging.

Although the company has an outstanding history of disciplined risk management on hedging, any disconnection between hedging positioning and volatile market conditions could cause Vistra to enter periods of earnings volatility and/or delay the connection between realized and reported profitability.

Also, Vistra has consistently missed revenue estimates over the past quarters due to management only providing guidance on adjusted EBITDA and FCF. However, consensus has constantly been focused on revenue, leading to repeated “misses,” which has been ruining investor confidence and may delay fair valuation.

Conclusion

Vistra is currently being mispriced by the market due to low investor confidence, the lack of announced hyperscaler and data center deals and also a focus on GAAP revenue, instead of focusing on the underlying cash and clear earnings visibility through 2027, which is also backed by disciplined hedging and contracting. The company's hybrid business model, which separates them from peers, provides more stable earnings than most merchant IPP peers and also gives them reduced power price swings that can affect their earnings. Vistra is bound to gain from the structural electricity growth in key markets, due to the demand from data centers, hyperscalers, and electrification, and has positioned itself as a key winner due to the recent strategic acquisition. Management also has a strong capital allocation strategy, focused on strong share buybacks, balance sheet deleveraging, and enhanced investments, which will improve share value even more. While regulatory uncertainty, deal timing, and deal follow-through create near-term risk, these factors primarily only impact investor sentiment and do not actually undermine Vistra’s long-term strong cash flow outlook. At current valuation levels, Vistra appears as a highly volatile strictly merchant generator with little to no hedging despite evidence of improving earnings supported by a very compelling and strong risk-reward profile and a good upside to intrinsic value.

data.title: Canadian Utilities: Catalysts Amid Modest Dividend Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since I last wrote about Canadian Utilities Limited (CU:CA) in May 2025, the company has posted a respectable total return of over 18%. After several years of sluggish growth, Canadian Utilities stock is benefiting due to several catalysts including the development of data centres in its operating jurisdiction and the advancement of some large electrical and gas transmission projects.

Operating in Canada, Mexico and Australia, Canadian Utilities is part of the ATCO Ltd. (ACO.X:CA) Family of companies, which owns a majority equity stake in the company. Canadian Utilities main operating utility group "ATCO Energy Systems" is the largest gas distributor in Alberta with 1.3M gas customers and 265,000 electrical customers.

Canadian Utilities is best known to investors as one of the preeminent dividend aristocrats in Canada. Although the company has slowed its pace of dividend growth in recent years, Canadian Utilities offers an unmatched dividend growth streak in Canada with 54 years of consecutive annual increases. With a 4.2% dividend yield, the utility continues to offer an steady source of cash payments to investors. The cash flow that underpins these dividends is backed by EBITDA generated from utility operations that are approximately 90% to 95% regulated.

Q3 2025 Results

For the first nine months of 2025, Canadian Utilities has delivered strong results, significantly improving on the company's performance over the first three quarters in 2024. Earnings attributable to shareholders increased from $316M in the year to September 30 2024 to $447M in the same period in 2025. This improvement is attributable to both operating segment improvements and losses on swap commodity contracts the dragged on earnings in 2024.

On the operations side, ATCO Energy Systems delivered adjusted earnings of $98 million in the quarter, $4 million higher year-over-year. The company's ATCO Australia division delivered adjusted earnings of $27 million during the quarter. This is $12 million or 80% higher than the same period last year.

Stock Performance and Valuation

Over the past 12 months, Canadian Utilities achieved total shareholder return of 33%, slightly ahead of the S&P/TSX Composite Index return of 32%. Over the same 12 month period to January 15 2026, the S&P 500 returned 16%. I don’t recall many periods where a boring utility has outperformed the broad indices.

Canadian Utilities is currently trading at 18.3x earnings, this level represents a 20% premium to the company’s 5-year average valuation of approximately 15.3x. Part of this rerating reflects broader sector trends as regulated utilities are seen as safe havens during market uncertainty. This multiple expansion also prices in near term growth prospects including some large projects coming on line in the next few years.

Favourable Operating Jurisdiction

In Q3 2025, Alberta’s population reached 5 million people. With a 2.25% growth rate, Alberta continues to lead population growth among Canada’s provinces and territories. Atco Energy Systems, Canadian Utilities main electric and gas operating company has an approved ROE of 8.97% for 2025. The company operates through seven distinct lines focusing on electric, gas , transmission and distribution. In the first 9 months of 2025 91% of the company’s revenue was derived from its Alberta operations. The Alberta Utilities Commission [AUC] is broadly seen as an accommodating regulator in the Canadian utility landscape. According to Fitch Ratings:

In Alberta, the AUC uses various regulatory mechanisms to bolster long-term financial stability. The AUC has a history of achieving multiyear rate increases in base rate proceedings, although with lower-than-average allowed return on equity [ROE] compared to U.S. peers. Fitch observes lower authorized ROEs across Canada than in comparable U.S. peers. However, CUL's Alberta utilities have a long track record of earning healthy returns, consistent with or exceeding U.S. peers.

Growth Plans

The company’s latest capital plan calls for investments of up to $6B in regulated utilities over the next three years. This capital growth is anticipated to drive compound annual growth of 5.4% on Canadian Utilities’ rate base.

The $2.9B Yellowhead Mainline natural gas pipeline is scheduled to begin construction as early as mid-2026 and start operations in late 2027. This project links the Northwest Alberta natural gas supply region to the Greater Edmonton area, home to Alberta’s industrial heartland. Reflecting the growing industrial demand for natural gas in this region, the Yellowhead pipeline project is already 90% contracted. On the company’s Q3 2025 earnings call, Canadian Utilities CEO, Robert Myles updated investors on the projects recent milestones:

We are pleased to announce the approval of the needs application from the Alberta Utilities Commission, or AUC, I'm also excited to announce that we filed the facilities application with the AUC earlier this week, which will provide detailed technical, environmental and consultation data required for construction approval.

In the fourth quarter, contracts for major equipment and steel pipe were awarded for this project and the remaining capacity has been marketed. The company also moved forward with its general rate application to the AUC seeking approval for Yellowhead pipeline revenue requirements of $381M, $489M and $680M for the years 2026, 2027 and 2028, respectively.

The Central East Transfer-Out project or CETO is a $280M project aimed at upgrading and strengthening the electrical transmission system in Central East Alberta. This project is targeted at addressing the congestion in the grid making energy transmission more efficient to Alberta’s major population centres. Canadian Utilities expects CETO to be in service in the first half of 2026.

Benefiting from the Alberta Data Centre Initiative

In December 2024, the province of Alberta, launched a plan outlining how the jurisdiction intends on attracting data centre investments in the province. Alberta’s AI data centre strategy, Powering the Future of Artificial Intelligence, argues that the province is an ideal location for data centre development based on its energy-only electricity market, low corporate tax rates and cold climate, which makes cooling more efficient. As of late 2024, there were 239 data centres in Canada, 22 of which are in Alberta. As one of the leading operators of Alberta's electrical transmission network, Canadian Utilities is well positioned to support this demand growth.

Dividend Streak Continues - Growth Rate Slows

Earlier in January, the company announced a quarterly dividend of $0.4623 per common share payable in March 2026. This modest 1% increase to the dividend is a continuation of the deceleration in dividend growth Canadian Utilities has been advancing in recent years. While the growth is slower, the record of dividend increases remains unappareled in Canada.

The recent dividend increase announcement marks the company’s 54th consecutive annual increase. Given the moderate payout ratio and slow dividend growth, investors can interpret this dividend growth profile as an indication of the company’s stable cash flows and near term opportunities to deploy capital to growth projects.

The modest dividend growth rate is also reflected in the company's relatively conservative payout ratio. Despite the ramping up of major capital projects in the last two years, Canadian Utilities been able to keep its dividend payout ratio stable. Canadian Utilities has a payout ratio of 55%, compared to Canadian-listed utility peers, Fortis Inc (FTS:CA) and Emera Inc (EMA:CA) with dividend payout ratios of 71% and 77% respectively.

At current levels, the dividend yield is 4.2%, approximately 19% lower than the company's 5-year average yield. This decrease in yield reflects the strong share price performance in 2025.

Risk Analysis

Canadian Utilities is domestically focused and regionally regulated. The vast majority of revenue is generated in the province of Alberta. These characteristics help to insulate the stock from the geopolitical risk factors that weigh on sectors more affected by international trade and investment.

Despite the Alberta Utilities Commission broadly being considered to govern a constructive regulatory framework for utility operators in Alberta, the regulator recently required Canadian Utilities to refund $71M to customers for failing to demonstrate cost savings to customers under the company’s performance commitments.

Interest rate risk is another key risk that all utility operators contend with. Not only do higher interest rates raise the cost of capital for utility operators with high leverage, higher available interest rates make the stock’s dividend yield less attractive relative to fixed income alternatives.

Relative to peers, Canadian Utilities has slightly elevated leverage with 6.1x FFO leverage. This compares to approximately 5.9x for Fortis Inc. (FTS:CA) and 5.8-5.9x for Emera Inc (EME:CA). As of Q2 2025 Canadian Utilities and its subsidiaries have access to approximately $3.6B in committed credit facilities with approximately $2.4B in available liquidity. In addition to sufficient liquidity, Canadian Utilities has been successful in raising cash to finance its capital program. According to CFO Katie Patrick:

On September 8, Canadian Utilities Limited announced a $750 million transaction of hybrid notes at a fixed rate of 5.45%. And on September 11, CU Inc. announced a $370 million transaction of debentures at a rate of 4.787%. I am proud to say that these offerings had significant interest from the investment community at approximately we're 3x oversubscribed across a strong pool of buyers.

This is a positive indication that the company will be able to raise the capital required to fund the Yellowhead project with a combination of 63% regulated debt and 37% regulated equity.

Investor Takeaways

Canadian Utilities is advancing major capital projects that will support a growing rate base. This highly regulated utility operates in a constructive regulatory environment and is poised to benefit from continued population growth and growing demand for residential, industrial and data centre power needs in Alberta. While dividend growth has slowed to token levels, the current dividend yielding 4.2% remains well covered.

With the strong share price performance valuation above historical levels, I have recently taken some profits in Canadian Utilities. I continue to hold a position and plan to reinvest dividends. With the proceeds from the recent trimming of my position, I added to peer utility - Fortis. While I still like Canadian Utilities for its Alberta exposure and regulated business model, I prefer Fortis at this time for its superior dividend growth profile.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Hyatt: There Are Definitely Stronger Options In The Segment
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When I stayed at a Hyatt Hotels Corporation (H), I remember my impressions: The atmosphere, the service, the luxury. Given the emphasis on the premium segment and the experience I gained researching other hotel stocks, I expected to see a positive assessment for my investment portfolio from Hyatt Hotels Corporation shares. Instead, the output of my quantitative analysis were somewhat disappointing, resulting in H stocks receiving a negative sell rating.

Thesis Explanation

I've been using a quantitative approach to stock analysis for a long time. I've developed my own stock evaluation system, which consists of eight parameters. If the overall rating exceeds 70%, the stock can be added to a long-term portfolio. If the rating is below 50%—that is, the financial performance, financial position, and growth factor scores are only half-perfect or worse—I try to avoid such stocks.

My approach showed that Hyatt is only strong in the growth area, while profitability and financial position are rather poor. Since growth metrics dominate my weighted rating, positive double-digit growth figures could boost the overall value, but beyond that, I found nothing compelling in the financials or the company. The overall rating is less than 50% of the benchmark for an outstanding stock.

Hyatt Is Just Moving To An Asset-Light Model

Unlike Hilton (HLT), Hyatt began its transition to a franchising model 10 years later. This is the main fundamental reason for the lag on financials, in my opinion. Recently, the company has been actively selling assets, while remaining a hotel operator under a long-term management agreement. According to 2024 reports, the company received more than $1.6 billion by selling assets such as Hyatt Regency Aruba Resort Spa & Casino, Hyatt Regency Orlando and others.

As of Q3 2025, the owned segment's share ~37% of all direct revenue, while Hilton's share is even lower (~24%).

Despite a solid gross profit margin of 42%, the quarter was very weak, with a loss of $50 million. This resulted in net income TTM being negative for the first time since COVID. This loss is largely due to two factors.

First, over the last four quarters, no gains from sales that would be considered non-recurring in financial analysis were recorded. And once the effect of one-time sales disappeared, the true state of the income statement was revealed.

Secondly, I want to note the high debt burden. Average debt over the past 12 months, according to my calculations, exceeded $5.3 billion, with shareholders` equity just over $3.5 billion for the same period. This significant debt load resulted in $282 million of the $485 million operating income TTM (or 58%) being spent on debt servicing. That's too much.

Due to the net loss, neither ROE nor ROIC nor earnings quality are available for analysis, so in my opinion, the company's financial performance and financial position are among the worst in the sector and are not sufficient for including the stock in a long-term investment portfolio.

Ascending Phase Of The Growing Cycle

While everything seems really dire for now, there are some positive aspects.

Taking into account the sale of some hotels and the transition to a management model, as well as the industry-wide growth cycle and organic room growth, the three-year EPS CAGR was a significant 38%. It's rare for this figure to be higher than the five-year cycle one (~20%).

Given the ongoing transition to an asset-light model, we can expect margin growth and an expansion of the company's premium portfolio. Analysts expect EPS to approach $4.37 by December 2027, representing a CAGR of more than 70%.

But I should notice that the asset-light model is more effective in larger chains, where fixed G&A costs are spread over a larger volume. Currently, Hyatt is much smaller than Hilton (~300k rooms versus ~1.5m). That's why I consider these forecasts to be at least optimistic.

The second important point: When we talk about growth rates, it's important to remember that 2020 and 2021 were very challenging for the industry, and the company suffered significant losses. Therefore, using three- and five-year metrics, we're now facing a low base effect.

Sales of some hotels also complicate fair valuations. For example, on Dec. 30, it was announced that Hyatt completed a $2 billion sale of Playa's owned real estate portfolio to Tortuga. I'm not sure how exactly this deal will be reflected in the reports and what impact it will have on quarterly and annual results. But at a minimum, it will definitely distort net operating efficiency.

Bottom Line

To summarize, we're seeing an interesting situation. I've settled on a sell rating, but this is not a recommendation for active sell or short. It's more of a cautionary tale and my own tactic of avoiding H stock. Why?

Because I'm currently observing a transitional phase in the business, but not like SAP, for example (I wrote about this here). Margins are declining, debt is weighing heavily, and the current financial position remains at least average.

Against this background, all-time highs seem unjustified.

On the other hand, there are decent profit forecasts and clear benefits from the transition to franchising and management with a focus on the premium segment.

The main risk of my thesis is a positive scenario of transition to a lightweight model, a rapid recovery of margins, and, as a result, high net profit. Then, both debt and profitability metrics will return to normal, and my rating will be unjustified post-factum.

Hyatt is more closely tied to luxury and lifestyle brands than any other US hotel chain. This segment is expected to grow faster than average in the coming years. Even with economic fluctuations, premium leisure and high-end business travel remain resilient. Barring a recession, Hyatt could post unexpectedly strong quarters.

But I still suggest holding off on buying shares, as buying solely on the prospects in an industry like the hotel business contradicts my cautious strategy for building a long-term investment portfolio. Pandemics or military conflicts can set back business development by years, and currency fluctuations are a daily risk factor for shareholders in such a sector. Therefore, for H stock to be a strong candidate, I'd like to see a clear signal of success in the transition to an asset-light model. While I may risk buying the stock higher and higher, I want to be confident I'm buying an outstanding business. I'm nowhere near that certainty right now.

data.title: Impax Large Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

The US Large Cap portfolio’s underperformance versus the S&P 500 Index was primarily attributed to challenging security selection across key technology-related holdings. Questions around investments returns on capital expenditures related to AI, and AI’s potentially negative impact on software as a service, pressured stocks such as ServiceNow (NOW), Tyler Technologies (TYL), Oracle (ORCL), Microsoft (MSFT) and Netflix (NFLX). The team retains strong conviction in these companies and the durability of their competitive advantages. Selection was also weak in Industrials and Materials as investors gravitated towards more speculative, risky securities and away from higher quality companies with proven track records. Within Consumer Discretionary there was a clear bifurcation in fundamentals as the higher- income consumer continues to be resilient while the lower-income consumer is showing weaker spending trends. The portfolio’s exposure to strong brands and resilient end markets drove positive security selection within the sector. Selection in Real Estate was also a tailwind as the portfolio’s exposure to the strong secular opportunity related to senior living was rewarded as Ventas reported continued occupancy growth and higher rental income. Regional bank Citizens Financial Group was another strong performer, as the stock benefited from a steepening yield curve and strong fundamentals.

Market overview

Equity markets, as measured by the S&P 500 Index, ended the quarter modestly higher. The period was marked by bouts of volatility, driven by concerns over elevated valuations and uncertainty surrounding returns and financing for large-scale data center investments. These factors prompted profit-taking in certain mega-cap technology and AI-related companies, although the market overall remained resilient. Market leadership shifted towards Health Care, which rallied following the Trump administration’s agreement with major pharmaceutical firms to reduce Medicaid drug prices. Pro-cyclical sectors also outperformed, with Materials buoyed by commodity strength, and Financials expected to benefit from looser monetary conditions. Small caps were also positive reaching new highs, as investors seek out attractive valuations.

Softer labor market data and signs of consumer weakness in residential housing led the Federal Reserve (Fed) to deliver two additional interest rate cuts during the quarter, despite persistent inflationary pressures. While some economic indicators softened, US Q3 GDP growth surprised to the upside, and corporate earnings remained robust. A new Fed Chair in 2026 is likely to maintain a ‘dovish’ stance, with further rate cuts highly likely throughout the year.

As we enter 2026, our macroeconomic outlook is one of cautious optimism. Falling interest rates and rising government spending should provide support for risk assets. However, narrow drivers of economic growth and unsustainable fiscal trajectories remain areas of concern. Within equities, we continue to take a critical view of certain valuations and expect investors to refocus on fundamentals and seek opportunities in companies whose potential has been overlooked. Our investment approach continues to emphasize businesses with strong growth drivers, sound management and attractive valuations, enabling us to build well-diversified portfolios that are differentiated from the broader market.

As of 05/01/2025 prospectus, total annual Impax Large Cap Fund operating expenses, gross of any fee waivers or reimbursements (excluding Acquired Fund fees and expenses), for Institutional Class and Investor Class shares are 0.73%, 0.98%, respectively.

Sector Attribution

Only two sectors outperformed the index during the quarter: Health Care, which benefited from its defensive characteristics and attractive valuations that drew inflows during periods of market uncertainty; and Communication Services, which was supported by Google’s AI breakthroughs and potential acquisition activity involving Warner Brothers Discovery. Financials delivered mixed results, with capital markets outperforming traditional lenders after the December rate cut. Information Technology (IT) experienced challenges in December due to ongoing concerns about elevated AI-related capital expenditures, while Industrials gains were concentrated primarily in segments like logistics, aerospace and defense. Consumer Discretionary performance was divided, with areas supporting higher income consumers showing some resilience. Consumer Staples underperformed as questions around organic volume growth for many companies in the sector weighed on investor sentiment. Materials were driven by metals and mining equities, reflecting sustained strength in industrial commodity prices. Real Estate, typically sensitive to interest rates, might have been expected to benefit from easing rate expectations but continued to face headwinds related to long-term financing concerns. Utilities experienced profit-taking after electric utility stocks delivered strong returns over the calendar year.

The portfolios sector tilts were a marginal detractor in the period, with the overweight to Health Care, and underweights to Consumer Staples and IT contributing positively. The underweight to Communication Services was the largest detractor followed by the overweight allocations in Real Estate and Industrials.

Contributors

Danaher (DHR) (Health Care, Life Sciences Tools & Services) delivered strong Q4 performance driven by robust demand for bioprocessing and diagnostics products, supported by continued strength in pharmaceutical and biotechnology end-markets. Margin expansion from cost efficiencies and successful integration of recent acquisitions further boosted earnings, reinforcing investor confidence.

Meta Platforms (META) (Communication Services, Interactive Media & Services), which is not held in the portfolio, declined due to weaker advertising revenue growth and rising expenses tied to its continued investment in AI infrastructure and the metaverse, which pressured margins. Additionally, cautious guidance for 2026 and concerns about slowing user engagement in key markets contributed to investor pessimism and a broad tech sector pullback.

Agilent Technologies (A) (Health Care, Life Sciences Tools & Services) delivered solid Q4 2025 performance supported by strong demand in pharmaceutical and biotechnology end-markets, particularly for analytical instruments and consumables. Operational efficiencies and disciplined cost control helped offset macroeconomic headwinds, driving margin stability and reinforcing investor confidence.

Detractors

Alphabet Class C (GOOG) (Communication Services, Interactive Media & Services), which is not held in the portfolio as the investment team owns the Class A shares, was one of the top performers in the period. The rising share price was driven by robust advertising revenue growth, particularly from YouTube and Search, as digital advertising spending rebounded during the holiday season. Cloud revenues saw double-digit growth and margin improvement, while cost optimization initiatives boosted profitability, leading to positive investor sentiment.

Oracle (ORCL) (Information Technology, Systems Software) was decidedly weaker in the quarter, as investors weighed strong stock performance in the third quarter alongside concerns over rising competition in cloud infrastructure and margin pressure from heavy AI-related capital expenditures.

Uber Technologies (UBER) (Industrials, Passenger Ground Transportation) was down due to weaker-than-expected ride-hailing demand in key markets and rising regulatory scrutiny over driver classification, which raised cost concerns. Additionally, higher insurance and compliance expenses pressured margins, while investor sentiment cooled amid slower growth in delivery services.

Outlook

Looking forward, the outlook for US equities is marked by cautious optimism. The Federal Reserve’s policy decisions are under close scrutiny, with a growing consensus among market participants that additional interest rate cuts may be forthcoming by mid-year should inflation continue its downward trajectory. While the labor market remains relatively robust, early signs of normalization are emerging, which could help alleviate wage-driven inflation and bolster corporate profit margins. Against this backdrop, we are monitoring several key forces shaping the economic landscape: technological innovation, shifting consumer preferences, broader societal changes, and evolving regulation and policy. The team believes companies that have consistently delivered solid earnings growth, yet remain undervalued relative to peers, are well placed to outperform as investor focus returns to fundamentals and valuation.

The investment team believes the market continues to underestimate the growth opportunities coming from long-term secular trends. While the market’s recent focus has been narrowly focused on artificial intelligence and interest rate expectations, the team sees very attractive opportunities and valuations that significantly discount the longer-term growth rate for companies addressing these secular trends. The team’s focus remains on identifying companies with secular tailwinds, durable competitive advantages and attractive valuations. Looking into 2026, the team believes the portfolio is very well positioned to capitalize on secular opportunities while holding well-managed companies with the proven track records and strong balance sheets to provide ballast in volatile market environments.

data.title: AGNC: Strong Investment Setup In 2026
data.description:
AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) managed to grow its portfolio of MBS in the fourth quarter and delivered high single-digit growth in its tangible book value compared to the year-earlier period. If federal fund rates head lower in 2026, the mortgage REIT could be set to benefit from a strong revaluation catalyst for its mortgage-backed securities portfolio, in my opinion.

AGNC continued to grow its net interest income rapidly in the fourth quarter (10%+), which highlights robust core investment profitability. While shares are not cheap, trading at a premium of 34% to GAAP book value, the market clearly expects AGNC to perform well this year. With more rate cuts on the horizon and inflation being less of a threat than last year, AGNC maintains a strong investment setup for investors looking for income and book value growth.

Previous Rating

AGNC was a Strong Buy for me previously (October 2025) -- Strong NII, BV Growth, Upside -- mainly because of the potential for a mortgage-backed securities repricing in a falling-rate world. Mortgage-backed securities, or MBS, are bond-like instruments, meaning they can be expected to do well for institutional investors if the Federal Reserve lowers interest rates. I like AGNC as a revaluation play with regard to its mortgage-backed securities portfolio, and I see a year of strong book value growth for the investment firm, especially if the Federal Reserve were to accelerate its rate cut trajectory in 2026.

Strong NII Gains in the Fourth Quarter

AGNC’s investment portfolio consists chiefly of Agency mortgage-backed securities that have the implicit guarantee of the U.S. government against credit losses, meaning investors are protected from borrower default risk. The mortgage REIT owned $81.8B in mortgage-backed securities at the end of the fourth quarter, showing 6.2% quarter-over-quarter growth. Because mortgage-backed securities increase in value during times of falling interest rates, they are attractive investments to buy when interest rates can be expected to head lower.

AGNC did not only report strong growth in its portfolio (and book) value, but also delivered robust results for the fourth quarter in terms of actual investment performance: the mortgage REIT’s interest income grew 10.3% year-over-year to $944M while interest expenses declined.

As a result, AGNC’s net interest income grew 79% year-over-year, and the REIT benefited from 8X faster growth in net interest income compared to its interest income. The implication is that ANGC’s investment business is set for stronger profitability in a falling-rate environment, which is exactly what I expect in 2026: after the Federal Reserve lowered interest rates by 25 basis points in December, more rate cuts are highly likely this year. I expect the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates at least 2-3 times in 2026, potentially setting AGNC up for even stronger net interest income growth in the quarters ahead.
