Due to strong NII performance and higher net interest income, the mortgage REIT's economic return bounced back in the last two quarters as well: the economic return surged to 11.6% in the fourth quarter, the highest level since Q4 '23.

AGNC’s Valuation and Book Value Growth

Shares of AGNC are currently priced at a 34% premium to book value, which compares to a 24% premium for shares of Annaly Capital Management (NLY), which has not yet reported results for its fourth quarter. Both mortgage REITs effectively trade like bonds in the stock market as their underlying mortgage investments react inversely to changes in interest rates. Therefore, mortgage-backed securities are uniquely suitable investments for investors that expect falling interest rates.

On the opposite end of the mortgage asset investment spectrum are mortgage servicing rights, which increase in value during rising-rate periods, largely because the underlying fee stream becomes more valuable over time. This means that investors should invest in mortgage servicing rights when federal fund rates rise and buy mortgage-backed securities when rates are about to fall.

While shares of AGNC now trade at a significant premium to book value -- the historical P/B ratio in the last three years is just 1.06X -- the prospect of falling interest rates in 2026 supports an investment, in my opinion. I also recommend Annaly Capital, as the mortgage REIT platform has seen improving core earnings strength and last year raised its dividend as well: 1.04X Coverage, Strong BV Growth, Cheap.

In terms of book value, AGNC has seen a robust rebound in its trajectory in the last two quarters, highlighting that the investment setup for mortgage REITs has in fact greatly improved. AGNC's net book value increased to $8.88 per share in the fourth quarter, showing 7.2% Q/Q growth. With more rate cuts on the horizon this year, I believe AGNC and Annaly Capital could both see significant (~10%) growth in their book values in 2026.

Risks With AGNC

The biggest risk for the mortgage REIT is that the Federal Reserve takes its time with cutting interest rates in 2026, which would delay an important repricing catalyst for the company’s MBS portfolio. What would get me to potentially change my mind about AGNC is if the Federal Reserve were to start hiking federal fund rates, which currently runs counter to consensus expectations. This could happen in an accelerating U.S. economy or if inflation were to make a comeback. As I explained here, the odds are in favor of strong U.S. GDP growth in the fourth quarter, which could potentially affect the Fed's rate-cutting timeline.

Final Thoughts

AGNC submitted a decent earnings report card for the fourth quarter that highlighted strong net interest income growth, higher net interest spreads, and high single-digit book value growth amid a favorable investment environment for mortgage-backed securities.

Both AGNC and Annaly Capital are directional plays on falling interest rates in 2026, and I would expect a gradual improvement in the investment environment this year. While shares of AGNC now trade at a significant premium to book value, I believe the market has adjusted its expectations lately, especially after the Federal Reserve lowered interest rates by 25 basis points in December.

With more federal fund rate cuts in the cards this year, AGNC has a strong investment setup and could drive its book value significantly higher on the back of a repricing of its rate-sensitive mortgage-backed securities.

data.title: National Vision Holdings: I Don't See Market-Beating Returns Now (Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in December 2023, I decided to dig into National Vision Holdings (EYE), a rather interesting company that, at the time, had been experiencing some pain because of the termination of its partnership with Walmart (WMT). At that time, the company was generating significant revenue from that arrangement. But because of how that relationship was structured, the overall impact on the bottom line was relatively minimal, or I guess I should say outsized in a way that did not severely affect the company. Despite this issue that the company experienced, I viewed the picture in a bullish light. Management had a plan for future growth, and shares were trading on the cheap. At the end of the day, I had no choice but to rate the company a "Buy."

The trajectory since then has been quite positive. Even though we have seen some volatility, revenue, profits, and cash flows have all generally trended higher. The number of locations that the business has in operation increased, and future growth looks promising. The stock price has reflected this, with shares rising 48.9%. However, it is worth noting that this still puts the company slightly behind the S&P 500, which has generated a return for investors of 51.1% over that same period. This is the kind of performance relative to the market that is more in line with a "Hold" rating. And even though the company has done well, shares are more expensive now than they were back then from a valuation standpoint. In light of this, I do believe that downgrading the stock to a "Hold" is the appropriate course of action.

I see limited upside

Whenever I go a long time without analyzing a company, I'd like to take a step back and look at its overall business model. This helps to re-familiarize myself with the enterprise. Operationally speaking, National Vision Holdings is an interesting business. The company operates as one of the largest optical retailers in the United States. They do this through a few different brands. They own two of these, the first being America’s Best Contacts and Eyeglasses, and the second being Eyeglass World. However, the company also has what it calls host brands. These are brands that it essentially manages. They include Vista Optical locations that are in certain Fred Meyer stores, as well as Vista Optical locations that are situated on military bases.

Management has long promoted the company as a key player in the value segment. This is for the budget-constrained consumer, which should actually make it quite appealing in the current economic climate when consumer confidence is near all-time lows. For instance, at its America’s Best locations, it offers up two pairs of eyeglasses, as well as an eye exam, all for $89.95. Two pairs of eyeglasses can be purchased for $99 at its Eyeglass World locations. To be honest with you, these numbers are shockingly low. The place that I go for glasses costs me between $200 and $400, depending on where I get them. So this does mean real value for shareholders relative to what other options are available.

Conceptually, this is a large market opportunity for the company. According to management, the optical retail market in the United States was worth about $70 billion as of 2024. However, it is a space that's continuing to grow. By 2030, they estimate that it will be worth $85 billion or more. And currently, National Vision Holdings operates as the third-largest player out there. However, that still means that it has a market share of only about 2.6%, which means that this is a highly fragmented industry that offers the opportunity for significant growth by acquisition or by muscling out smaller competitors that are unable to compete with low costs. And that's where this business truly shines.

Long term, the company sees multiple growth vectors that it can tackle. This includes underdeveloped customers, which would be those that fall under managed care, those that are outside prescriptions, and the like. It includes the underdeveloped products category, such as frame premiumization, anti-reflective offerings, transitions, progressive lenses, and the like. It includes improving the customer and patient experience through the use of data-driven segmentation, the integration of e-commerce and customer relationship management activities, and more. And it also involves new store growth that targets new markets and that invests in new formats.

Management is confident that it can continue to play in the value category while also pushing for higher prices. For instance, it believes that, through product innovation and price increases, it can raise product-only revenue by between 1.5 and 3 times as time goes on. That is significant in and of itself. But of course, there is a fine line to walk here, since consumers are budget constrained and price sensitive to things like this. We already see, for instance, that while its share of lower-income customers is comparable with the share of those who are low income in the United States, it does cater to a larger share of middle-income individuals. I actually do find this to be rather interesting considering that the value play should apply more to those who are lower income. But that suggests to me that the ability to hike prices, unless doing so purposely targets the higher-income individuals that are underrepresented in the company's revenue stream, could be limited.

Over the last few years now, management has done a fine job of capitalizing on the opportunities that exist. This has shown up in the form of revenue. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years. Revenue expanded from $1.64 billion to $1.82 billion. This growth has come about pretty much entirely by its America’s Best brand, with revenue shooting up from $1.37 billion to $1.56 billion. This can be chalked up to an increase in the number of locations from 905 to 1,036.

By comparison, other areas for the company have either stagnated or declined. In the chart below, you can see overall location count for the company, including by vertical. This includes results through the first nine months of 2025 relative to the same time of 2024 as well.

We continued to see growth associated with America’s Best, with an increase in store count to 1,049 locations. That's up from the 1,019 locations that the company had a year earlier. Eyeglass World, meanwhile, has seen a continual decline in location count since 2023, while the other segments for the company have either flatlined, or in the case of its Fred Meyer locations, dropped lately.

Profitability for the company has been rather mixed, as one of the previous charts illustrates. But the good news is that, for 2025, things are looking up. As the chart above illustrates, revenue expanded to $1.48 billion during the first nine months of 2025. That's up from the $1.39 billion reported the previous year. Overall profitability surged, while cash flows rose nicely. During this period, the company benefited significantly from comparable store sales growth. In fact, when it came to the business as a whole, this was 5.7%, with America’s Best rising a hefty 6.7% year over year.

Management attributed this to higher average ticket prices at a time when customer traffic was essentially flat. This is interesting to me, but not necessarily surprising. When you are one of the cheapest players out there, or perhaps the cheapest, and you operate in a space where what you provide is essentially needed by a large chunk of the population, you can hike prices so long as your price is still comparable to or lower than the competition. This would obviously have a huge positive impact on profitability as well, which is precisely what we have seen.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety, management projected revenue of between $1.97 billion and $1.99 billion. At the midpoint, we would be looking at revenue of $1.98 billion, which would represent an 8.5% increase year over year. The firm is on track, based on the results achieved for the first nine months of the year, to generate EBITDA of around $182.4 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $148.3 million. Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company achieved in 2024, we can see in the chart above how shares of the business are currently priced. These are not bad multiples by any means, but they do put the business well in the fair value category as I describe it. They are certainly more expensive than they were when I last wrote about the business. In that chart, however, I did show that, compared to competitor Warby Parker (WRBY), which is admittedly not the perfect comparable given its e-commerce-oriented business model and its partnership with Google parent Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) aimed at providing smart glasses, shares are considerably cheaper. But I do chalk that up more to business model disparities than an indicator of actual value that investors can capitalize on.

Long term, we should expect continued growth from the company. For years, management had been growing at a rate of about 60 locations per year. This occurred between 2019 and 2023. But for the 2024 through 2027 years, it has been targeting slower pace of growth, with an average of about 30 stores per year. However, between the investments the company is making in innovation, changes in its competitive position through pricing, and the like, it expects to resume the more accelerated store growth path of 60 locations per year between 2028 and 2030. The fact that management has confidence in this is certainly encouraging, though we will have to wait and see if this expansion materializes.

Takeaway

Based on all the data provided, I would argue that National Vision Holdings is an interesting prospect. But it's not one that I find to be the most compelling. The company should continue to grow nicely, though shares are not as cheap as I would like them to be. It is in light of this valuation that, even though I like the business from a growth perspective, I believe that downgrading it to a "Hold" makes sense.
